column22:
I am a licensed italian physiotherapist and pain coach specializing in neuroplastic pain conditions. Since 2021 my work focuses on helping people who experience persistent symptoms such as fibromyalgia, chronic back/neck pain, migraines, dizziness, tinnitus, and other conditions where the nervous system has become sensitized.


My clinical approach is grounded in modern pain neuroscience and the biopsychosocial model of pain. I integrate Pain Neuroscience Education (PNE), mind–body techniques such as Emotional Awareness Expression Therapy , EFT Tapping , somatic awareness, and other nervous system regulation strategies to help individuals retrain the brain’s threat response and reduce chronic symptoms. Rather than focusing solely on structural explanations for pain, I work with patients to understand how their brain and nervous system can generate real symptoms even when no tissue damage is present.


My interest in this field began through my own experience with chronic pain. After struggling with persistent cervical pain for several years and trying many conventional treatments without lasting results, I discovered the growing body of research on neuroplastic pain (Dr. Sarno) and mind–body medicine. Applying these principles to my own recovery led to a profound change in my symptoms and inspired me to dedicate my professional work to helping others who feel stuck in similar situations.

I am the founder of the NeuroBalance® method, an educational and therapeutic framework designed to help italians recover from chronic symptoms . The approach combines neuroscience education, emotional processing, somatic tracking, and behavioral retraining.
I work primarily online with clients internationally and collaborate with other health professionals to support a multidisciplinary approach when appropriate.
I am listed as a practitioner on the Pain Reprocessing Therapy website and continue to study and integrate emerging research in chronic pain, neuroplasticity, and mind–body medicine.

Jacqueline holds certifications in pain reprocessing therapy, applied positive psychology, health coaching, yoga, and other complementary therapies.

Her personal journey with chronic pain led her to passionately explore this work with a deep resolve to help others. Various therapies such as pain relief psychology, somatic therapy, positive psychology, and polyvagal informed therapy are integrated to create an individualized treatment approach to restore mind, body, and nervous system health.

Jaime Henle is a licensed psychologist and provides individual therapy for adolescents and adults (aged 13 and up). She specializes in trauma and chronic pain. She is trained in Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) and Trauma Focused Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (TF-CBT) which are both evidenced based treatments for trauma. She utilizes the mind-body work of John Sarno, MD and Nicole Sachs, LCSW and completed the Sarno Sachs Solution Practitioner training. Their work believes that your brain and nervous system create pain symptoms in order to aid in the repression of unconscious thoughts and feelings. The goal of this work is to express repressed emotions which will help your nervous system go from fight or flight to rest and repair. Dr. Henle specializes in: trauma and bereavement (physical, sexual and verbal abuse, and neglect), attachment issues, anxiety, depression, chronic pain, body image concerns, adjustment issues, substance abuse disorders). Dr. Henle also wrote and illustrated five children’s books, one of which relates to mind-body work: Why Does my Body Hurt? A Children’s guide to the Mind-Body Connection, and how Repressed Emotions Lead to Physical Pain (can be purchased at Lulu.com)

Dr. Henle provides in person sessions in Evans (Augusta area, Georgia). In addition, she provides telehealth sessions and is licensed to practice in New York and has obtained an Authority to Practice Interjurisdictional telepsychology (APIT) from PSYPACT (This includes 42 participating PSYPACT states).

See this link to see the 42 effective states: https://psypact.org/mpage/psypactmap

I support children ages nine and up, teens, and adults.


I am a Pain Reprocessing specialist, as well as an Emotional Awareness and Expression Therapy (EAET) practitioner. I have also completed years of training in Attachment‐Based Intensive Short‐Term Dynamic Psychotherapy (AB‐ISTDP).


I weave neuroscience, emotional insight, and relational depth into care that feels both grounded and hopeful. Whether I’m teaching adolescents how the brain learns (and unlearns) neuroplastic symptoms, guiding them toward the feelings underneath anxiety, or designing creative exposure activities, including one unforgettable anti‐zombie mission that helped an adolescent client reclaim the outdoors. I bring a mix of specialization, humor, and tailor my services to each client's individual needs.


My approach is collaborative, practical, and evidence‐based; clients often come to me feeling stuck or confused by their symptoms, and together we build clarity, confidence, and the courage to move through fear and avoidance toward a values‐based life.

column22:
My journey into treating chronic pain and other chronic health problems began well before I went to physical therapy school in 1995. I was dealing with my own social anxiety, different pains, sinus infections, and digestive issues over the years. Little did I know that I was about to go on a healing journey not only for myself, but to help others as well.

It started with my traditional physical therapy training at Emory University in Atlanta, Georgia. I entered the PT field treating all sorts of pain in outpatient clinics. At the time, I was taught to think mechanically and structurally, that the body was a machine. However, I quickly realized that the techniques and exercises I gave people seemed to have a little correlation with how they did long-term. People seemed to either get better or they didn’t. I couldn’t understand why some people would improve and why others would linger in pain, even though I was providing the same treatments for similar timeframes. In the clinic we would get chronic pain cases coming in and we didn’t know what to do with them. I became frustrated. Frustrated with my inability to really understand and help my patients and frustrated with my own pains and anxiety that wouldn’t go away. Something in me said there has to be an answer to this. So I embarked on a mission to learn, heal myself ,and in the process to be able to help others heal as well.

In 2001, I decided to go to Australia and pursue a second Masters degree in Physiotherapy. This is where I first learned about the intricacies of pain science and how the brain and nervous system play a big role in pain. I was lucky to train with Peter O’Sullivan, who is a well-known physiotherapist in our PT world. At the time we would study core stability as that was all the rage then. The thing was, these studies did not show core stability was the answer to low back pain. Pete has since gone on to start an approach I trained in called Cognitive Functional Therapy, which incorporates bio-psycho-social principles to treat persistent pain. While in Australia, I also had the opportunity to learn from two other well-known physiotherapists Lorimer Moseley and David Butler. Both these guys have written books and have done extensive research in the pain field.

In 2002 upon returning to the U.S., I was introduced by another PT to Vipassana meditation. I had meditated a little before but decided to jump in and see what this was about. This was no 30 or 60 minute meditation training. Vipassana requires you to sit for 10 days at 8-10 hours a day with no talking. I called it a mental marathon. It was not easy by any measure but I did it and learned a lot about my inner state. I went on to do three more 10-day silent retreats and multiple 3-day and 1-day practices as well. Out of Vipassana, I became interested in other types of meditation. When I was on the island of Bali, I practiced for 3 days a form of shaking meditation which was done in standing and was unique to the teacher who started it. I also completed a MBSR (Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction) course with Jon Kabat-Zinn, as well some other meditation retreats with meditation teacher Adyashanti and writer Eckhart Tolle. I found all forms of meditation helped to cultivate an inner awareness, which ultimately shapes my current approach to treating chronic pain and other chronic health ailments. You don’t need to meditate, you just need to learn how to build and shift your awareness.

Through a colleague, I came across the work of Dr. John Sarno, a M.D. in New York City. I heard Dr. Sarno was getting people with physical ailments better with a psychological approach. I ordered and read all of his books and became intrigued with what he was doing as it made sense to me. I reached out to him and he told me that I should go see one of his colleagues Dr. Schechter in Los Angeles. I spent a day learning from Dr. Schechter, M.D. and how he works with patients. Dr. Schechter has an excellent book “Think Away Your Pain” and a good Mind-Body Workbook.

I then was fortunate enough to train and learn from Dr. Howard Schubiner, M.D. who is one of leading physicians carrying on and advancing the work of Dr. Sarno. I have been to Detroit to work with Dr. Schubiner and have trained in his classes multiple times as well. He has three very well written books (Unlearn Your Pain, Unlearn Your Anxiety & Depression, Hidden From View) and has undertaken multiple research studies looking at the mind-body connection and chronic pain. Howard has been a huge influence on my work.

Dr. Schubiner turned me on to a fellow physiotherapist in England, Georgie Oldfield, who started doing this work and was teaching others. I’ve been to the UK three different times to train and learn from Georgie. Georgie has formed an organization called SIRPA (Stress Illness Recovery Practitioners Association) and she has written a book Chronic Pain:Your Key to Recovery. I have become a SIRPA practitioner through her work. Georgie continued to show me how the mind can truly affect the body and how thoughts and emotions have the power to produce pain and other physical symptoms in the body.

As I delved deeper into the mind-body connection, it sparked my interest to gain alternative perspectives on healing. My professional training as a PT was limited to traditional western medicine. Over a period of about 3-4 years, I combined my love of travel with a desire to learn about various alternative healing methods. I spent a lot time in Sydney, Australia learning Acuenergetics, an eastern-based approach that mirrors acupuncture using your hands. I learned about Chinese medicine, the meridians, and the energy system within us. I was in Adelaide, Australia learning from a Chinese healer who used an obscure technique of bamboo sticks to tap and beat on the body to create a healing state. I trained from traditional Maori healers in New Zealand who used a form of deep hands-on bodywork to rid the body of pain. I spent a month in India outside Chennai learning an ancient hands-on healing practice based on what they call Siddha medicine. I travelled to Canada multiple times to train in a form of energy healing called Bio-Energy. I went to Sedona, Arizona to become certified in Bio-Dynamic Breathwork, a form of breathing practice that is used for healing. I have also spent time in other trainings as well (ISTDP, Havening, etc.).

I have literally travelled around the world looking and searching for the best ways to heal. I can tell you everything I have learned helps some people to some degree some of the time. Each has merits in its own right. But what I realized after all these trainings, time, travel, and money spent was something that brought me full circle back to where I started. It was not the technique that mattered, it was what happened in myself or the patient that mattered. That was the one commonality in all this. There are a thousand treatments out there, but there is only one result that happens in people when the feel better. Simply the brain and body relax. The brain shifts to a more calm state, the nervous system moves into the parasympathetic mode, people feel supported and safe, and their bodies shift as a result.

Healing is not done by someone else or by some technique. Healing is an inside job and when you can 100% realize from your mind that you are ok in your body, that you are safe, that you are not under threat, that you do not need these protective and guarding responses any longer, what happens is your system starts to relax. Then you start to feel better. It is getting to this point that is the practice.

The healing journey is more of an unwinding and letting go than actually doing anything to heal. In actuality there is nothing to heal with chronic pain and chronic conditions. The body is healed. It is reconciling the mind with this notion. Stop trying to heal and start realizing you are ok now, your body is not broken, and that this is just brain and neural signaling gone awry.

I had to come full circle in my healing journey. I’m telling you now that you don’t have to take the long road like I did. I can give you shortcuts to get the heart of the matter quickly so you can get moving forward now. Please reach out with any questions or send me a consultation request to get started.

I am a licensed professional counselor and adjunct instructor at Naropa University specializing in trauma, chronic pain, and nervous system dysregulation. My work is grounded in mindfulness- based and evidence-informed approaches, including Pain Reprocessing Therapy (PRT), Somatic Experiencing (SE), Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT), Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR). I support clients in transforming their relationship with pain, fear, and internal experience - helping them move from patterns of avoidance, hypervigilance, or shutdown into greater presence and resilience. My own lived experience with chronic illness informs my work with deep empathy and attunement. I offer a compassionate, non-pathologizing space for adults navigating the complexities of suffering and healing.


I provide virtual therapy throughout Colorado and offer pain coaching to those worldwide.

column22:
Being sick or in pain is stressful. Being sick or in pain for a long time is extremely stressful. Being sick or in pain with no end in sight can be debilitating - in mind, body, and spirit. My own experience with chronic health conditions left me stuck. I couldn’t find satisfying answers or helpful solutions. I felt unheard or misunderstood when talking with medical providers. Although I felt hopeless at times, I kept looking. After much searching and suffering I found a perspective and tools that allowed me to recover and return to a full life. When symptoms return, I know what to do.


It is because of my personal experience I am passionate about working with others who suffer with chronic health conditions or pain, known as Neuroplastic Symptoms. While I customize my therapy approach to each unique client, there are similar elements. First, we discuss information about the mind body connection and neuroplasticity. Together we decide what elements of Neuroplastic Symptoms may fit for you. No shaming. No gaslighting. Next, we explore what may be going on at a deeper level and how to address it. As a therapist specializing in trauma and complex trauma, I feel equipped to address many issues that may be connected to neuroplastic symptoms.


I would love to share more about my approach. I am currently accepting new virtual and in person clients in Minnesota. I am licensed in Wisconsin and can meet with clients virtually. Please reach out today for a Free Consultation!

Jodi K. Silverman, LCSW, LICSW is a licensed clinical social worker with over 30 years of experience specializing in trauma, chronic stress, and mind–body conditions. She is currently integrating neuroplastic pain and pain reprocessing approaches into her work, helping clients shift their relationship to symptoms and restore safety and trust in the body.


Jodi is the founder of TheraChi Healing, where she works with adults experiencing chronic pain, complex trauma, and stress-related conditions. She has particular expertise in supporting individuals recovering from relationships with antagonistic or emotionally harmful people, with a focus on rebuilding nervous system stability and embodied self-trust.


Her integrative approach combines evidence-based psychotherapy with Brainspotting and nervous system–informed modalities, including the Safe and Sound Protocol (SSP), Rest and Restore Protocol (RRP), HeartMath®, and Reiki-assisted psychotherapy. She emphasizes compassionate, regulation-first care to support neural retraining and whole-person healing.

Judi received her Masters in Social Work from Columbia University and certificate in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis from The Training Institute for Mental Health in New York City. She is a faculty member with The Center for Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis of New Jersey.

Judi’s interest in chronic pain has been ongoing throughout her career as she has treated many patients with psychophysiological disorders. Judi treats patients with a combination of Pain Reprocessing Therapy, relational psychodynamic and mindfulness techniques. She believes that uncovering repressed anger, trauma and split off emotions is the key to understanding and thus recovering from chronic pain and psychophysiological disorders. Judi also specializes in the treatment of eating disorders, anxiety and depression.

Juergen Schmider MD, PhD, Doctor Medicinae Habilitatus, is a Clinical Pharmacologist (licensed in Germany), who has practiced in psychiatry and
Behavioral Therapy (Max Planck Institute for Psychiatry, Tufts University, Charité) including pain medication, and chronic pain diagnosis and was a

Hi, my name is Karen Ash, and I am an ICF-ACC certified coach (International Coaching Federation) who founded Migraine Oasis to support individuals navigating the migraine-specific expression of neuroplastic pain and associated symptoms.

I have lived with migraine for nearly four decades and bring both lived experience and professional expertise to my work. My background includes a decade in pharmaceutical management, with direct exposure to the clinical trials and launch of the first CGRP medication to market. Through my own healing and training in neuroscience-based mindbody approaches, my understanding of pain, chronic stress, unprocessed emotional patterns, and the nervous system fundamentally shifted toward addressing underlying drivers rather than symptoms alone.

My coaching integrates pain neuroscience education, nervous system regulation and resilience, and evidence-informed brain retraining approaches (Neuroplastic Recovery Therapies), trained specifically in Pain Reprocessing Therapy (PRT), Dr. Dan Ratner’s Columns System, and currently enrolled in EAET Training (Feb 2026). I support clients in reducing fear, building a felt sense of safety in the body, and reconnecting with meaningful, engaged lives.

I am a proud member of the ATNS Coaches’ Advisory Council (CAC) and an active ATNS member. I work with an international clientele and currently split my time between Basel, Switzerland, and Naples, Florida (USA).

My name is Kendra Dixson, LMSW-C; I am a psychotherapist - clinical social worker, independently licensed in the state of Michigan. I have been practicing psychotherapy since beginning my Master of Social Work program in 2010, at Wayne State University. Since graduating in 2014 with my master’s in social work, I have provided psychological evaluation and therapy for hundreds of individuals, struggling with a wide range of psychiatric disorders, including Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder, depression and anxiety, as well as issues related to domestic violence, substance abuse, separation from loved ones and dislocation.

My work experience includes working for over nine years as a psychotherapist at an internal medicine clinic at Detroit Receiving and Sinai Grace Hospitals. From 2019-2024, I served at Sinai Grace Ardmore Clinic; an out-patient, community mental health agency providing psychological evaluation and therapy for persistent and pervasive mentally ill patients. Since 2019, I have also maintained a private practice in which I provide psychological assessment and therapy for adults, dealing with a variety of psychiatric disorders.

Over the years I have conducted hundreds of clinical evaluations for a wide variety of clients. Additionally, I have done advanced training: 1) Trauma informed therapy, the Mind Body Syndrome utilizing Emotional Awareness and Expression Therapy modality; and 2) The Immigration Evaluation Training Center in best practices for conducting evaluations for various immigration cases, including Asylum Cases, VAWA Cases, and Petitions for Extreme Hardship
Waivers.

Psychology does affect physiology! Your pain is real! You are  not making it up! It would be cruel to think otherwise, so what  is pain? It is a protector, a warning signal that detects danger.  All pain is produced by the brain. The brain is incredibly  

complicated! We only use ten percent of the conscious  mind, and ninety percent is controlled by the subconscious  and unconscious mind. Your pain is being produced from  your subconscious and unconscious mind. Pain is an  Emotional experience. Repressed emotions from past  trauma and current stressors can produce real physical pain  or other symptoms in the body.  

We will determine together, if you may have neuroplastic pain  from a compassion and empathic approach. I am trained in  evidence-based research for chronic pain: Emotional  Awareness and Expression Therapy, Pain Reprocessing  Therapy, and Cognitive Therapy for Chronic Pain. Please,  view on YouTube the following information regarding this form  of treatment: -Changing Your Mind: Chronic Pain- and the  documentary trailer for -Pain Brain-and -This Might Hurt-. I  believe you can improve your heath by treating yourself with  these treatment methods, and I will show you how.  

I have over 25 years of clinical experience working with  seniors, veterans, and adults struggling with serious mental  illness. I am a former chronic pain sufferer myself. I have  received my Bachelor's Degree from the University of  Michigan and my Masters Degree from Wayne State  University. Please, feel free to contact me regarding this form of  treatment and for resource information.

column22:
Dr. Husman has been practicing psychiatry independently since 1999, completing board certification in psychiatry in 2001 and child and adolescent psychiatry in 2002. She then spent 4 years working in East Central Iowa completing her assignment with the National Health Service Corps, followed by serving 15 years as a partner at Cedar Centre Psychiatric Group, providing both inpatient and outpatient care. She joined Eastwind Healing Center in 2020, drawing upon a wide range of experiences over the past decades, and she knows what services work well in her hands and what populations she feels best equipped to serve. Her focus is working with women and children and LGBTQIA+ individuals ages 6-64 with anxiety, trauma, and pain conditions. 

Dr. Husman is certified in Comprehensive Energy Psychology, Holographic Memory Resolution, Acceptance and Integration Training, clinical hypnosis with ASCH, and Pain Reprocessing Therapy. She is a certified Neurodynamic breathwork facilitator, Certified International Health Coach and Certified Trauma-Informed Coach. She also is a certified psychedelic assisted therapy provider and provides ketamine for treatment resistant conditions in the office. These tools promote relaxation in various ways, can assist in providing proof of safety, and allow for changes to occur at the unconscious level. Self-compassion training, self-guided imaginal exposure, cognitive reframing, empty chair Gestalt technique, many Ericksonian techniques, and resetting or clearing the mind-body though tapping and other means are all components in use, and these tools can be used together as is called upon to provide a unique experience for the patient. These tools, taken as a whole, are best used for the treatment of all types of anxiety and trauma, in addition to Tourette’s, stuttering, ADHD, and depression and chronic pain.

Sometimes, our best cognitive intentions are thwarted by our unconscious mind frozen in all those moments of shame, regret, anger, humiliation, fear, and the like. Sometimes our judge and critic will step in to stop us from forward progress. This is being human! Homework is often involved, and unwillingness to do personal homework will typically result in slower recovery, so she will work with the patient on blocks to this as well. It is a team effort! In hypnotherapy, she agrees with the sentiment that all hypnosis is self-hypnosis, and she will create recordings to support the patient in mastering this capacity. In essence, moving away from the hypnosis of dissociation and fight/flight/freeze/fawn, to a new capacity of empowered self-regulation. Dr. Husman facilitates processing gently, and most often indirectly, so the patient is comfortably in control of the process.

Please be aware that because of working with the unconscious, memories of traumatic events may fade, meaning not only the emotional charge. If you are involved in a legal proceeding related to a past traumatic event, you may wish to discuss working with the unconscious with your attorney before proceeding.

column22:
Lauren is a psychotherapist, coach, and chronic pain survivor based in Los Angeles, CA.

Her personal healing journey revealed the mind’s powerful ability to change and adapt, igniting her passion to support others in their mind-body healing. She’s successfully guided numerous patients through diverse chronic pain challenges and physical symptoms, with a focus on providing relief for those struggling with dizziness. In client sessions, she is not just a quiet listener. She actively guides clients with reflections and feedback, ensuring an interactive therapeutic experience. She helps clients navigate the intricate connections between individual, family, community, and societal systems that shape our well-being. It is through this lens that she empowers clients to explore what is happening in their minds and bodies in a genuine and compassionate way. If any of this resonates with you, check out the link to her website or send her an email for a free 20-minute consultation call. She looks forward to learning about your story.

column22:
About
Leslie Coates Burpee, MA, LPC, is a licensed and board-certified clinical mental health counselor at Expand Psychology. She works with adults navigating anxiety, depression, trauma, OCD, relationship challenges, and chronic health concerns. Leslie creates a supportive, collaborative space where clients can deepen self-understanding, build resilience, and move toward meaningful, lasting change. She draws from Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), psychodynamic therapy, Internal Family Systems (IFS), mindfulness, and solution-focused techniques to tailor treatment to each individual.

Specialization
Leslie specializes in treating anxiety, depression, trauma, OCD, and chronic pain. She has advanced training in Pain Reprocessing Therapy and frequently works with individuals experiencing physical symptoms that persist despite normal healing, often rooted in emotional stress. Her integrative approach combines evidence-based methods with deep attunement to the emotional and physical dimensions of her clients’ experiences. Leslie is an active member of several professional organizations, including the American Counseling Association (ACA), Virginia Counselors Association (VCA), Northern Virginia Licensed Professional Counselors (NVLPC), and the Anxiety and Depression Association of America (ADAA).

Education, Training, and Professional Experience
Leslie earned her undergraduate degree from Davidson College and a Master’s in Psychology from Harvard University, where she collaborated with Dr. Ellen Langer on groundbreaking research in mindfulness. She went on to complete a Master’s in Clinical Mental Health Counseling at Marymount University and received advanced training in CBT under Drs. Aaron and Judith Beck. In addition to her clinical work, Leslie supports early-career clinicians through training and supervision, providing both individual and group supervision, and contributing to the development of training modules for doctoral externs and counseling residents.

Dr. Lilia Graue is a physician, psychotherapist, educator, coach, and consultant in Mexico City, where she's practiced, lectured, and mentored for over 20 years in both Spanish and English. Her approach is collaborative, trauma sensitive, healing centered, and mindfulness and compassion based. She brings together radical care approaches to foster healing, and mentors healthcare professionals in engaging critical and feminist perspectives to promote justice and equity in medicine and healthcare.

In addition to her work in the field of chronic pain and other nociplastic symptoms, Dr. Graue has extensive experience in eating disorders and medical family therapy. She is part of the Medical Advisory Board for The Better Mind Center.

She lives with her partner and two cats, and in addition to her work she enjoys spending time with family and friends, baking, swimming, reading, traveling, and Sundays at the Philharmonic.

Linda Spain is a Washington State Licensed Mental Health Counselor, board certified in biofeedback, with a specialty in the field of behavioral medicine. Clinical experience includes over twenty years’ working with adults, adolescents and children treating stress-related disorders, including two years in the stress management clinic at Seattle Children’s Hospital and Medical Center.

In addition to her clinical work, Linda has served as a clinical instructor, teaching and training health care professionals in biofeedback principles and applications.  Since 2013, she has been a guest lecturer within the University of WA Physical Therapy doctoral program, on the use of surface EMG in treating chronic pain.  She currently serves as a professional mentor for those seeking certification in biofeedback.   She is a past president of the Biofeedback Society of WA and is a member of the Association for Applied Psychophysiology and Biofeedback and the Psychophysiologic Disorders Association.

Counseling sessions incorporate a cognitive-behavioral focus with treatment addressing issues identified by the client and working toward problem solving.  Sessions which include biofeedback, run 45 to 50 minutes in length, during which, non-invasive sensors are attached to monitor physiological activity. Various techniques are taught for controlling physiological states, with the individual provided with real-time feedback throughout the session.

column22:
My name is Lihi Lisser, I am a clinical social worker (M.S.W) and a psychotherapist. I am working with clients in Israel and virtually. I have been working in the field of mental health for about 15 years, both in public and private settings. But it wasn't until I had my own chronic pain experience that I got interested in the mind body connection and how it is related to both mental and physical health. Years of suffering from chronic pelvic pain, trying to figure out how to treat it, and trying every biomedical based treatment possible led me to Dr. Sarno's books as a kind of "last resort". This was the beginning of my own recovery process, which not only changed my personal life, but also changed completely my professional approach. For about a year, I did everything I could to educate myself about the mind body model. As a part of my training, I participated in both practitioners' courses led by Dr. Schubiner (Freedom from Chronic Pain and EAET). Using this approach not only helped me reach full recovery from my own chronic condition but also filled me with the confidence, motivation and hope to help others in their recovery journeys.

Nowadays, I work with people presenting with all kinds of chronic pain, and I dedicate a lot of my energy to trying to spread the word and educate other professionals in my field about this model. I integrate EAET and PRT into other modalities like psychodynamic psychotherapy, IFS, cognitive behavioral and somatic techniques. Based on this knowledge, as well as my own recovery experience, I try to tailor a treatment plan that meets each client's specific context and unlocks his or her own barriers to recovery.

Dr. Payne’s research focuses primarily on identifying neurobiological, behavioral, and psychological biomarkers related to the transition to chronic pain, particularly in adolescents and young adults with menstrual pain. Clinically, Dr. Payne provides psychological care to individuals with all types of chronic pain and symptoms using Pain Reprocessing Therapy, Emotion Awareness and Expression Therapy, and related mind-body approaches. She additionally specializes in
treatment for women with pelvic and gynecologic pain, including chronic pelvic pain, bladder pain, endometriosis, and vulvodynia.

Lynn Crothers is a psychotherapist in private practice in Western North Carolina. A former board member of ATNS and former chronic pain sufferer, she lived for years with chronic migraines, cluster headaches, and other unexplained neurological symptoms. This lived experience fuels her work. Her practice is dedicated in large part to serving adults living with chronic pain and other chronic symptoms, with an emphasis on the correlation between trauma and physical suffering. Trained in the depth work of relational psychoanalysis, she also at times incorporates mindfulness, somatic practices, skill building, and the science of neuroplasticity.

Currently, she is accepting psychotherapy clients in North Carolina and Washington state. Later in 2025, she will expand to include coaching. Please reach out if you’d like to learn more.

Dr. Matt McClanahan is an osteopathic physician dual-boarded in Family Medicine and Neuromusculoskeletal Medicine, and holds a masters in Exercise Physiology. His diverse experience spans primary care, osteopathic manual treatment, integrative medicine, addiction care, ISTDP, psychedelic-assited therapy, and end-of-life support. He holds both basic and mastery certifications in Pain Reprocessing Therapy (PRT) and has completed the Apprentice level certification in Psychedelic Somatic Interactional Psychotherapy.

His passion for treating chronic pain and mind-body syndromes was sparked in 2012 through work with Dr. Howard Schubiner during his residency. In 2018, "Dr. Matt" left a primary care practice and founded the Center for Insight Medicine, a consultative clinic specializing in the diagnosis and treatment of neuroplastic symptoms. He is also senior faculty with the Freedom From Chronic Pain training group and from 2023-2025 served as the physician lead for the PRT arm of the PUPPY Trial - the follow-up trial to the original PRT ("Boulder Back Pain") study.

He lives and practices in Chattanooga, TN.

column22:
Laura Haraka is a pelvic pain specialist, Somatic Experiencing Practitioner, breathwork facilitator, and mind-body coach dedicated to helping individuals heal chronic pain and nervous system dysregulation. Her work is rooted in her own healing journey—after years of living with chronic pain, Laura discovered the power of nervous-system-based and neuroplastic approaches, somatics, and emotional healing. As she learned to reconnect with her body, cultivate safety within, and release protective patterns like perfectionism and fear-based vigilance, her symptoms transformed.

Inspired by her personal recovery, Laura founded Feel to Heal Wellness, where she specializes in supporting people with pelvic pain and neuroplastic symptoms through Somatic Experiencing, breathwork, nervous system education, emotional capacity-building, and mind-body neuroplasticity tools. Her approach emphasizes empowerment, compassion, curiosity, and the body’s innate ability to heal when given safety and connection.

Laura is also the host of the Beyond the Pelvis podcast, where she shares both expert insights and powerful lived-experience success stories highlighting the mind-body connection, pelvic pain recovery, trauma healing, and nervous-system science. Through her work, she is dedicated to helping others reclaim hope, trust in their bodies, and experience true mind-body healing.

I am a registered clinical psychologist, currently offering services through a virtual private practice. I have both personal and professional experience working with a wide range of neuroplastic symptoms. I use a combination of PRT, EAET, and IFS approaches in my work. I feel very privileged and encouraged to be able to offer neuroplastic treatment to clients, as it can create life-changing impacts.

I completed my Bachelor of Arts, Honours degree in Psychology at the University of Winnipeg in Winnipeg, Manitoba, and received my Ph.D. in Clinical Psychology from McGill University in Montreal, Quebec. I have previously worked in the Rural and Northern Program within the Department of Clinical Health Psychology at the University of Manitoba, the Cardiac Rehabilitation Program of The Ottawa Hospital and the University of Ottawa Heart Institute, and served as the Director of the Education Directorate of the Canadian Psychological Association, overseeing the association’s national accreditation and continuing education programs.

I have also had the opportunity to spend some time living abroad, where I was involved with multiple non-profit organizations dedicated to supporting the health and well-being of women and children. Additionally, along with my business partner, Dr. Karen Dyck, I am the co-founder of Intentional Therapist, a continuing education initiative designed to support the self-care of female mental health therapists.

column22:
Are you struggling with chronic pain, anxiety, or depression that seems impossible to shake? I specialize in helping caring, conscientious adults—especially during pregnancy, postpartum, and other high-stress transitions—overcome symptoms that doctors often can't fully explain.

As someone who has personally recovered from chronic pain using mind-body approaches, I understand how terrifying and isolating these symptoms can feel. I offer evidence-based treatments including Pain Reprocessing Therapy (PRT) and Emotional Awareness and Expression Therapy (EAET) to help you heal from chronic pain, IBS, vertigo, pelvic pain, and other neuroplastic symptoms.

I'm also a Certified Perinatal Mental Health Professional (PMH-C) specializing in pregnancy and postpartum anxiety, depression, and birth trauma. My approach integrates PRT, EAET, and EMDR in a warm, nonjudgmental space.

You don't have to keep suffering. Using your brain's natural neuroplasticity, we can help you overcome symptoms, build resilience, and reconnect with yourself. Located in Lafayette, CA. Offering telehealth throughout California.

column22:
I am a graduate of Wayne State University’s Doctor of Physical Therapy program and have been treating patients for over 10 years. I have spent most of
my career working in outpatient hospital settings seeing patients with orthopedic and neurological diagnoses.

Throughout my time as a practitioner, I have seen many patients with chronic pain. In my previous approach to physical therapy, some patients with chronic pain made significant progress, others showed only minor improvement, and some did not improve at all. I found these outcomes to be frustrating as a
provider, and I’m sure my patients who had shown little or no progress found this to be even more frustrating.

In my search for methods to help these patients, I became certified in pain reprocessing therapy (PRT) and completed the OvidDx program. This opened a
paradigm shift of treating the root cause of neuroplastic symptoms, and I am seeing the results in my patients’ progress.

My skillset as a physical therapist, including being able to recognize inconsistencies in movement patterns and with palpation, often provides the evidence a patient needs that structural changes are not the cause of their pain. The explanation of these findings and associated treatments has been instrumental in the recovery process for many of my patients.

I look forward to sharing my expertise and helping as many patients as possible find their path to recovery.

column20: Mind Body Medicine, Orthopedics, Somatic Coaching, Yoga Therapy
column22:
Michelle is a Physician Assistant in both Mind Body Medicine and Orthopedics and is also a Somatic Coach at Cormendi Health. She has over 2,000 hours of training in mind body practices, including yoga therapy, Pain Care Yoga, Trauma Informed Yoga, Pain Reprocessing Therapy, and body based psychotherapy. She graduated the top of her class at both University of Colorado Boulder and Rosalind Franklin University of Medicine and Science and quickly found her training in mind body healing was essential in her work as a Physician Assistant. Her training in Somatic Experiencing is the foundation of her approach to somatic coaching, offering an experiential body based approach to healing mind body symptoms.

Michelle has her own personal experience with pain, and that has been the fuel for her focused career on educating about pain neuroscience and leading patients in practices to increase their own capacity to heal pain. She has special interest in working with nervous system patterns in the body as a way to increase awareness and resiliency. At the heart of her work is the idea that creating a relationship with the body and using it as a resource can have a profound effect on mental, emotional, and physical health.

Breath and Body Sessions: Sessions with Michelle can take many forms depending on needs and goals, but all involve practice. Being with the experience of the body, we bring awareness to habitual nervous system responses that are present, experiment with strategies for altering those responses to better serve you, and make connections with how this informs your continued healing moving forward. Sessions may involve general body awareness, somatic tracking related to pain or how emotions or thoughts are experienced in the body, breathwork, relaxation strategies, or movement.

Michelle is a Physician Assistant, Somatic Coach, and Yoga Therapist with over 2,000 hours of training in mind body practices to promote healing. Her professional path is extremely influenced by her personal experience with pain, which fuels her deep passion for integrating mind body approaches with more traditional medicine in her practice. In addition to practicing in orthopedics, she offers mind body health coaching in the treatment of persistent pain and other chronic symptoms.

Michelle has trained in Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, Pain Care Yoga, and Trauma Informed Yoga and has special interest in working with nervous system patterns as they express in the body as a way to increase awareness and resiliency. At the heart of her work is the idea that creating a relationship with the body and using it as a resource can have a profoundly positive effect on mental, emotional, and physical health.

column20: Mental Health Counseling for overlap of chronic conditions and trauma.
column22:
Natalie Nibler Escobedo is a Licensed Professional Clinical Counselor (LPCC 6394) in the State of California. Natalie is also licensed to practice in Oregon,Colorado,Michigan, New York and New Hampshire. She has over 11 years of experience working with individuals, couples, families, and groups. She has a Master of Arts in Counseling Psychology with a focus in Organizational Consulting earned at Pacific University in Hillsboro Oregon. She received a Bachelor of Science in Psychology from Western Oregon University in Monmouth Oregon. She has worked in inpatient and residential treatment facilities for adults as well as adolescents in both Oregon and California. In those settings, she provided individual therapy, family, and group therapy services. Additionally, she has worked in the Employee Assistance Program (EAP) setting providing couples therapy, individual therapy, and crisis intervention. She has specialized training in crisis intervention, dual-diagnosis, addiction treatment, overlap of medical issues/neuroplastic symptoms and trauma work. Natalie has lived experience with rare genetic condition/disorder that aids her in further understanding her client’s experiences with medical complexity/medical trauma. She views her client’s symptoms from the Strategic Family Therapy lens; that roles are responsible for symptoms and when we repurpose/change roles, that the symptoms go into remission. While it is not always possible for awareness to be enough for full remission of change, she utilized Eye Movement Desensitization Reintegration (EMDR) therapy to aid in requiring the brains core beliefs that are negatively impacting the clients life utilizing the 8-phase Adaptive Information Processing (AIP) model.


Natalie has experience working with Anxiety Disorders, Attention Deficit Disorders (ADD/ADHD), Mood Disorders (Depression and Bipolar), Autism Spectrum, Dual Diagnosis (Substance abuse and a Mental Health Disorder), Eating Disorders, Adoption/foster care (Adjustment/Attachment disorders), Complex Post Traumatic Stress Disorder/OSDD, Military & First Responders, Couples therapy and complex cases with overlap of medically unexplained symptoms (neuroplastic symptoms).


She has received advanced training in the following evidence-based practice therapy models: Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR & EMDR 2.0), Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP) Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT), Solution-
Focused, Strength-Based, Strategic Therapy, Dialectical Behavioral (DBT), Developmental Meeting Needs Strategy (DMNS), Ego States and is Internal Family Systems informed. She is an EMDR Certified Therapist and also a Consultant- in-Training through EMDRIA. Natalie is passionate about continued personal and professional growth, as such, she regularly completes advanced clinical training.

column22:
Nicole completed her Physiotherapy degree in 2004 and developed an interest in pain early in her career as a neurological physiotherapist. Since 2012 Nicole has dedicated her practice to the lived experience of persistent pain and in 2018 was awarded a titled membership with the Australian College of Physiotherapists in pain management. As a Senior Mindfulness Facilitator Nicole’s practice is dedicated to both the science and experience of the Mindbody connection, specifically the interface between pain and suffering and the arising potential for insight and growth. 

Nicole's sessions employ a range of powerful tools, including, but not limited to, Pain Reprocessing Therapy (PRT), Cognitive Functional Therapy (CFT), Emotional Awareness and Expression therapy (EAET), and insights from Intensive Short Term Dynamic Psychotherapy (ISTDP). The primary goal is to facilitate self-discovery while nurturing safety, agency, and discernment, ultimately empowering individuals to reclaim control over their bodies and lives. Central to the therapeutic process is a strong foundation of connection, fostered through open communication, transparency, understanding, and acceptance. The exploration of emotions is done with a sense of playfulness and consent, allowing both therapist and client to embrace the intricacies of the human experience. Together, they confront challenges head-on, openly acknowledging and addressing the obstacles that arise. Nicole's mission is to guide clients beyond their pain, providing them with the tools needed to reengage with life fully.

column20: Mind-Body Counseling, Somatic Psychotherapy and Mind-body & Well-being Coaching
column22:
I am a licensed professional counselor with over 40 years of experience in mental health counseling and body-mind psychotherapy. I served as a professor of counseling psychology for 17 years and as the Department Chair of Somatic Counseling Psychology at Naropa University for 3 years. In addition, I am a board-certified dance/movement therapist and have studied directly with leaders in the mind-body/psychophysiologic disorder field, including Dr. Bessel Van Der Kolk, Dr. Howard Schubiner, Charlie Merrill, and Annie Hopper.

Having fully recovered from my own debilitating experience with ME/CFS and fibromyalgia, I now am able to embrace an active lifestyle, including climbing mountains and trekking in Nepal whenever possible. My personal journey informs my work, and I understand the unique challenges of overcoming chronic physical symptoms from both a professional and lived perspective.

I believe in providing a safe, supportive space for my clients to explore the areas in their lives where they feel stuck. My approach combines evidence-based therapies like Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT), and Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT), alongside pioneering work in the mind-body illness field. I draw from the teachings of influential practitioners such as Dr. John Sarno, Dr. Howard Schubiner, Dr. David Clarke, Alan Gordon, Christie Uipi, Peter Levine, and Nicol Sachs to help my clients move toward greater vitality, comfort, harmony, and health.

I believe that the key to healing chronic symptoms lies in learning how to effectively respond to physical discomfort. Helping my clients to understand psychophysiologic disorders (mind-body illnesses), how they work and teaching them to utilize effective tools from Pain Reprocessing Therapy (PRT) like somatic tracking and practicing outcome independence, leaning into Dr. Kristin Neff’s self-compassion work, utilizing mental rehearsal, and Dr. William Pennebaker’s and Nicole Sach’s therapeutic journaling frameworks, I help clients alleviate their suffering and reclaim their well-being.

At the core of my work is the belief that everyone deserves to experience joy, passion, connection, and agency in their lives. Achieving this requires meeting clients where they are, assessing what’s working and what isn’t, and collaborating to set meaningful goals, make incremental changes, and overcome obstacles. I empower my clients to become their own best advocates, make decisions that align with their values, and create lives they truly desire.

It is both an honor and a privilege to engage in this sacred work with each individual I support. I offer services online, over the phone, or in person at my home office in Denver, Colorado. In addition to supporting individuals with psychophysiologic disorders, I work with clients facing challenges such as anxiety, depression, relationship struggles, addiction, and trauma. With every session, I bring my heart, expertise, presence, and a deep commitment to my clients' well-being. For clients outside of Colorado and internationally, I provide virtual mind-body coaching to support their healing journey.

I offer free 15 minute consultations. Feel free to text or call me at 303-887-4118 or email me at nicolmcgough@gmail.com.

column22:
I’m a licensed clinical therapist (LCMHCA) based in the Appalachian Mountains of Western North Carolina. I specialize in supporting clients as they navigate their own experiences of body trauma, chronic pain, PTSD, and complex grief disorders. The work that I do with clients is rooted in the foundation of neuropsychology and is strongly informed by pain reprocessing therapy (PRT). As well as the interrelated connections between trauma, nervous system dysregulation, and the neuroplastic functions of the brain. I wholeheartedly believe in our body's ability to heal. column22:
You have done everything you were told to do. You have seen doctors, had scans, tried injections, physical therapy, medications, and alternative treatments. Yet the pain continues, and no one can fully explain why.


Over time, the pain begins to take over your life. Activities you once loved feel impossible. You monitor every movement, worried you might make things worse. The uncertainty can lead to anxiety, frustration, and even hopelessness.
Your pain is real, and the suffering it creates is real.

If this sounds familiar, you are not alone. And there is hope.


Pain Reprocessing Therapy (PRT) is a neuroscience based, mind body approach that helps retrain the brain’s pain pathways. Research shows that while chronic pain feels like it is coming from the body, in many cases it is driven by sensitized neural circuits in the brain. Just as these pathways can be learned, they can also be unlearned.


Through this work, you will learn practical techniques to calm the nervous system, break the pain cycle, and begin restoring confidence in your body.


My interest in this work is also personal. After recovering from chronic pain myself through mind body approaches, I now specialize in helping others do the same.

Call 615.219.9932 to schedule a free consultation and begin exploring a different path toward healing.

I am a Registered Social Worker who primarily works with clients who have chronic pain. I am trained and experienced in assessing and treating chronic pain and mindbody symptoms that have no structural cause, often referred to as Neuroplastic Pain, Centralized Pain, a Psychophysiologic Disorder, or Tension Myoneural Syndrome. I specifically utilize Pain Reprocessing Therapy and Emotional Awareness & Expression Therapy to support my clients in reducing and/or eliminating their pain. My therapeutic approach involves helping clients to understand how our bodies can develop chronic pain (and other mindbody symptoms), and what can be done to help break the cycle of these chronic symptoms.


Throughout my life I had always battled headaches, but in my mid-20s the headaches became so severe that I had to stop all physical activity and took a leave from work; I simply couldn’t make it through the day without being bedridden by pain. It was pain that wasn’t responding to any type of treatment or medication, so I felt lost and hopeless. While I was only able to find some therapies that helped manage the symptoms, it was only enough to get me back to work but not enough for me to be able to actually enjoy my life. I came across some chronic pain books that introduced me to the idea of neuroplastic pain, and through learning and training in these modalities I was able to recover from my chronic pain entirely. It has now become my passion to help others through the pain reprocessing and emotional work so that they can also get back to living their lives, without the burden of chronic pain or chronic mindbody symptoms.

I am a clinical psychologist and life/health coach passionate about empowering people to live the life they want. I enjoy working with all ages- children, teens, and adults. I have experience working with many different concerns/goals, however my areas of specialty include anxiety, obsessive-compulsive spectrum symptoms, sleep issues, and mind-body concerns (e.g., chronic pains, chronic fatigue, medically unexplained symptoms).

I have a strong research background as I have designed and conducted a number of research studies, especially during my Ph.D. training. This informs my therapy and coaching work. I utilize evidence-based therapies backed up by lots of research! I love using therapies based on Cognitive Behavioral Therapy, Mindfulness (e.g., Acceptance and Commitment Therapy), Brainspotting (neuroscience-informed therapy to resolve trauma and stressors), Somatic Therapies, Pain Reprocessing Therapy, and Biofeedback (HeartMath Certified). When clients are open to it, I sometimes incorporate spiritual and energy healing into the healing process.

In addition, I am passionate about providing culturally sensitive care. I am of Asian Indian ethnic background and have done research that shows that distress is experienced and expressed differently across cultures. In some cultures, it is also more difficult to reach out for help for emotional needs.

I have recovered myself from a number of PPD symptoms (such as chronic pains) with the use of therapies such as Pain Reprocessing Therapy, which has been a major source of inspiration, passion, and drive for me to pursue training in this field to help others who are currently struggling with these symptoms. I have incredible amounts of empathy towards my clients having experienced many of the symptoms first-hand.

Rebecca Stein received her Bachelors in Science with Phi Betta Kappa Honors in three years from The University of Georgia in Athens. Rebecca majored in Psychology with a minor in Child and Family Development. Her interest in the mind-body connection led her to obtain a Masters in Clinical Social Work from The University of Southern California in LA. In graduate school, she focused on adult mental health & wellness as an intern at The Relational Center and The Pain Psychology Center, emphasizing a broad range of somatic, cognitive therapies.

After graduation, Rebecca was successful in helping postpartum mothers at UCLA Medicine- Pediatrics Comprehensive Care Center focus on the mind-body connection to cope with anxiety and postpartum depression. Rebecca’s work also emphasizes the intersection of trauma and chronic pain, and the strong correlation between childhood trauma, addiction, and ADHD, allowing patients to find relief from chronic pain, medically unexplained symptoms and psychological suffering.

In her spare time, Rebecca volunteers to lead support groups and creative workshops for teens with chronic pain at Creative Healing for Youth in Pain (chyp). She enjoys running, yoga, nature, dance, painting, live music, reading, baking, watching movies, and connecting with friends and family.

Now accepting new patients! I welcome referrals from clients located in Massachusetts, Vermont, and Florida, where I am licensed to practice.

 

Rob Fox is a Licensed Mental Health Counselor, Certified Employee Assistance Professional and Licensed Alcohol and Drug Abuse Counselor.

Rob works 100% online. His practice is limited to seeing clients who reside in the states of Massachusetts, Vermont, and Florida.

Along with treating PPD, Rob has a specialty in treating clients with OCD utilizing IFS, EMDR, and AEDP Therapy.

Rob resides in Massachusetts with his wife and dog Sonny.

column20: I had chronic pain for over 20 years. Now I help others find their way out.
column22: I was a successful actor and radio announcer when I woke up to chronic symptoms that would alter my life for two decades. I tried absolutely everything and saw every doctor there was, to no avail. When I found out what my pain truly was and why I had it, I recovered. This was so mesmerizing to me that I realized that the world had to know. Millions of people are struggling unnecessarily. What the world has been taught about chronic pain and symptoms is outdated and incorrect – science shows us this. At that point, I quit my career as a TV/film and radio performer to pursue this field. The knowledge and work I do is based on my own 20+ years of chronic pain and symptoms. This is important for several reasons; I understand every facet of this work because I went through it myself, and because I tried everything to get free. I found the way out, but that was only after years of education, reading, researching, and testing.

column22: Hello there! I am a licensed professional counselor who owns and manages a private group therapy practice. Although I’m based in Pennsylvania, I practice virtually across many different states and also roadtrip a ton, taking sessions from my office on wheels. I came to this field with the intention of working with pelvic pain as a specialty before determining that I myself manage chronic pain and pelvic floor dysfunction, which was a happy accident. I’ve also had personal experiences with other neuroplastic pain conditions including chronic back pain, headaches, and TMJ before finding my own healing path to remission, and now spend happy time hiking and exploring without fear of relapse. Pain reprocessing therapy, cognitive behavioral therapy, and acceptance and commitment therapy are a few modalities I use in my eclectic work. Many of my clients are members of the queer community, have chronic pain, and identify as perfectionists or people-pleasers, and want to work on managing the intensity they bring to their lives to increase their self-compassion and lessen experiences of chronic pain and bodily tension. I  ove collaborating with other professionals, engaging in mentorship and counseling, and yapping about all things sexuality-related.

Dr. Susanne Schröder is a German physician, board-certified in Anesthesiology with additional certifications in Intensive Care Medicine, Naturopathic Medicine, and Mind-Body Medicine. She completed medical training in Germany, Australia, and Switzerland, bringing an international perspective to patient care. Dr. Schröder specializes in the diagnosis and treatment of chronic primary and nociplastic pain conditions as well as neuroplastic symptoms, also offering evidence-based Mind-Body Medicine interventions for chronic health issues.

Her clinical approach focuses on neuroplastic pain mechanisms, integrating Pain Reprocessing Therapy (PRT) and Emotional Awareness and Expression Therapy (EAET) within a structured biopsychosocial treatment plan. She also incorporates traditional Mind-Body Medicine techniques, including breathing and relaxation exercises, to empower patients in managing chronic symptoms. Dr. Schröder has trained with and continues to exchange knowledge with leading clinicians in the field, including Howard Schubiner. She is the founder and medical lead of Mind Body Doc, a private practice in Düsseldorf, Germany, offering specialized care for patients experiencing neuroplastic symptoms and
chronic pain. In addition to her private practice, Dr. Schröder maintains active work in intensive care medicine within the German public health system.

Dr. Schröder combines her professional dedication with a personal commitment to well- being as a passionate mother of two, bringing both expertise and empathy to every patient interaction.

column22:
I help people use psychological and emotional modalities to heal from chronic pain.

Most people don’t even know that’s possible… but you know it is.

However, you’re not convinced it’s possible for you. You’ve had symptoms for years. You’ve had countless letdowns with medical treatments. You’ve finally found mindbody medicine.

This must be the answer. But—you worry—is it?

The success stories profoundly inspire you. But they don’t sound like you.

  • Another birthday passed. Your symptoms remain.

  • Another summer’s coming. Your symptoms remain.

  • Another year since you first found Dr. Sarno…

What’s wrong with you?

You’ve been doing the work.

In fact, you’re always doing work.

  • You’re a good student.

  • You’re a great worker.

  • You’re self-motivated.

  • You finish checklists.

  • You get promotions.

  • They love you at work, even if you don’t love them.

  • You’re a perfectionist.

  • You’re a little obsessive.

  • When others lack motivation, you get the job done.

So you’ve already read TMS books. And neuroplastic pain books. You’ve journaled. You’ve somatic tracked. But you’re viscerally realizing… mindbody healing is different. Because you’re still hurting. Your body’s still so uncomfortable.

 

You’re right. Mindbody healing is different.

It requires a mindset shift so profound that some call it a rebirth.

It’s deeper than the pain and the symptoms. It’s your very core self.

It’s hard to see it on your own, but this is an invitation to change more profoundly than you realized.

You haven’t been allowed to be you. You’ve been invalidated for being you.

You’ve put your precious life’s energy into stress, demands, people-pleasing, fitting in, proving yourself, trying to be “perfect,” trying so hard—now, you’re utterly exhausted. Your nervous system’s spent.

And the person you’ve become on the surface… is not really you.

We need to find you.

 

Your body’s calling you home.

Together, we’ll learn to speak its language. We’ll listen. With guidance, you’ll understand.

If your recovery feels difficult, that’s because your life has been difficult.

You already know, you’ve been repressing more than you initially expected.

Now, you need help seeing the full picture. You’re on the right path. Don’t go it alone. Choose to do something different. Choose to ask for help. You’re coming up against old fears and defenses. You’re ready to let them go.

  • You’re ready to get specialized therapy, specifically for your exact concern.

  • You’re ready to start understanding the language of your body.

  • You’re ready to attune to your true self.

  • You’re ready, above all, to start feeling better.

Let’s work together. Let’s get to know what you really feel. Together, let’s get to know who you really are.

Let’s get you the support you need, and let’s get you out of chronic pain.

column22:
Sebastián is a licensed Physical Therapist from Buenos Aires University with 20 years of clinical practice and experience working on the reversal of chronic pain and symptoms associated withstress. He is dedicated daily to providing personalized care to patients of all ages who want to find a root solution to their persistent pain and other psychophysiological disorders such as gastrointestinal disorders, migraines, or symptoms without medical explanation.

He is currently part of an international and multidisciplinary research and dissemination team based in Spain, which aims to promote the bio-psycho-socio-spiritual paradigm in health, particularly in the treatment of chronic musculoskeletal pain.

He worked in conjunction with Encarna Espunya, a prestigious Spanish physiotherapist who was currently invited by the PPDA to speak in Spanish at the 2021 Congress.

He also gives group theoretical-practical seminars for patients with psycho-physiological disorders together with Dr. Lorena Frasier (a doctor trained in psychoneuroimmunoendocrinology of stress) in person in Buenos Aires city and also in online
format.

Sebastián taught since 2008 the concept of Dr. Sarno's TMS at the University of Buenos Aires and the Maimonides University. He taught classes on the mind-body relationship and the approach of Dr. Sarno for more than 6 years at Maimonides University through a new subject created for this purpose called "Psychosomatic Integration and New Paradigms."

The University of Buenos Aires officially declares in 2008 of interest an extra-curricular course on New paradigms in health that it teaches together with other health professionals in line with the new paradigms. The search for him began with a chronic neck pain that was diagnosed as two disc protrusions and that lasted for more than 15 years despite trying all available mechanical treatments. It was then when in 2004 he came across Dr. Sarno's books and began a new way of redefining his pain that led him to convince himself of the accuracy of the mind-body concept described as TMS by the good Dr. Sarno.

The search expands over the years and discovers the books and seminars of Dr. Howard Schubiner by whom he is particularly inspired, also the contributions of Dr. David Schecter, Dr. Scott Brady and other popular popularizers such as Georgi Oldfield and S. Orzanich among others.

In 2009 he contacted Dr. David Schecter who agreed to have the “Mindbody Workbook” translated into Spanish by Argentine ́s professional translators and reviewed by Sebastián himself, which is still accessible on Dr. Schecter's own page.

Despite the low acceptance of a Mind-body approach in the Argentine academic environment, Sebastián continues tirelessly studying and applying the concepts of Dr. Sarno along with other tools such as Mindfulness, EFT and Focusing among others. He focuses his treatment programs on Neuro-education, Journaling and stress regulation techniques and mind-body connection in face-to-face and tele-medicine formats (online via zoom preferably)

column22:
Hi, I’m Shira. I’ve lived with pain - and I’ve healed from pain. I know how lonely it can feel, and I also know that the path out is not about willpower, but about connection, safety, and choice.
I am a Registered Psychotherapist with the CRPO, in Ontario, along with being a single parent, homeschooler, and parent to a food allergy kiddo. I work with neuroplastic symptoms and primary pain, as well as anxiety, overwhelm, and burnout. I work with individuals and parents, including Highly Sensitive People, perinatal, homeschooling parents, and parents living with the complexity of food allergies.


I like to think of Pain Reprocessing Therapy as an ice cream shop: there are many flavours, and we choose what works for you. If expressive writing feels overwhelming, we may not start with that path. If focusing on the breath isn’t accessible, we find another way to work with your nervous system. If sensory grounding—like savouring a piece of chocolate— helps create safety, we lean into that. And sometimes I challenge you to try something that seems challenging. The way through chronic neuroplastic symptoms can wind and wend, and it unfolds in its own time.

I am trained in Pain Reprocessing Therapy (PRT), Emotional Awareness and Expression Therapy (EAET), Flash (Four Blinks), and am currently completing EMDR training. My home is in a you-centered approach, known as client-centered therapy (Carl Rogers), where you are the expert of you. I bring psychological knowledge, curiosity, and a willingness to learn alongside you. I draw from mindfulness, grounding, “this-and-that” thinking (holding multiple truths at once), humour, and inner-child and IFS-informed work and other tools.


I’m not afraid to challenge you—and I can take the heat if you’re frustrated with me. I see the commonalities across psychotherapeutic methods more than I see the differences. Across modalities, I’m most interested in sense-making: connecting the dots of how you came to be where you are, so you can choose a path forward that feels safer, more regulated, and less driven by fear or symptoms. If we’re not the right fit after meeting—or not the right fit right now—I’ll do my best to help you find someone who is. Making connections matters to me. For those seeking free or low- cost therapy (not necessarily PRT-trained), Canadians can explore options through several programs, including my own project, Find a Student Therapist (www.findastudenttherapist.ca).

column22:
Member of the ATNS Coaches Advisory Council (CAC)

 

Hi, I’m Simone.

I help people recover from chronic pain and other persistent neuroplastic symptoms by retraining the brain and restoring a sense of safety in the body.

I specialize in complex, long-standing symptom presentations—especially for people who have “tried everything” and are still searching for answers. A big part of my work is helping you not only calm your symptoms, but also gently reconnect with ease, play, and joy as real medicine for your nervous system.

 

WHO I WORK WITH

  • Adults with Chronic Pain (back, neck, pelvic, limb, abdominal, headaches, migraines, etc.)

  • Other persistent Neuroplastic Symptoms (dizziness, nausea, fatigue, anxiety-related symptoms, compulsive patterns, and more)

  • People who have lost access to joy, playfulness, and ease

  I coach in English and German, both online via Zoom and in person in the Seattle metro area.

  

MY BACKGROUND

For over 14 years, I worked in neurology-based manual therapy before expanding into neuroplastic symptom recovery coaching.

I am:

  • Certified in Advanced Pain Reprocessing Therapy (PRT)

  • Trained in Emotional Awareness and Expression Therapy (EAET)

  • A graduate of Dr. Howard Schubiner’s Freedom from Chronic Pain and Beyond Pain Education practitioner trainings (with Charlie Merrill)

  • Trained in life and mindset coaching, Positive Psychology, and additional mindbody, mindfulness, NLP, and hypnosis-based approaches

I’m also a mom of two teenage boys who have experienced a wide range of neuroplastic symptoms (crippling abdominal pain, full-body pain, headaches, dizziness, nausea, anxiety, OCD, and more). I’ve walked the path through medications, procedures, hospitalizations, pain clinics, and CBT—and I deeply understand both the exhaustion and the hope that come with this journey.

Discovering PRT and the Boulder Back Pain Study was a turning point for me. The idea of treating pain by retraining the brain made immediate, profound sense—and I’ve devoted my work to mindbody conditions and the neuroplastic “pain brain” ever since.

 

HOW I HELP YOU RECOVER

Your Toolkit for Reprocessing Symptoms & Cultivating Safety

True healing involves turning down the brain’s overactive alarm system. Together, we use tools that give your nervous system real evidence that it is SAFE to stand down.

1. Understand Your Symptom System – Pain & Symptom Education
 We start by exploring how chronic symptoms can be the result of a misfiring alarm system—like a hypersensitive car alarm going off in a light breeze.

➤ Why it heals: Seeing symptoms as a false alarm, not damage, reduces fear and catastrophic thinking. When fear decreases, the threat cycle loses its fuel.

 

2. Build Somatic & Present-Moment Awareness – Interoception & Mindfulness
 We gently practice noticing your body—feet on the floor, breath, subtle sensations—without judgment or analysis.

➤ Why it heals: This builds interoception and teaches your brain that sensations are information, not danger. Your internal alarm system begins to recalibrate.

 

3. Reduce the Fear of Sensations – Graded Exposure & Reappraisal
 Instead of bracing, resisting, or panicking when symptoms arise, we learn to turn toward them with curiosity, using what you’ve learned about neuroplastic pain.

➤ Why it heals: Shifting from “Oh no, it’s back!” to “Oh, interesting—that’s just a sensation” sends powerful safety messages to your brain and rewires the symptom–fear connection.

 

4. Expand Emotional Awareness & Expression – Emotional Agility
 We explore the emotions underneath the symptoms—frustration, resentment, grief, anxiety from perfectionism or people-pleasing—and learn to feel and express them safely.

➤ Why it heals: Unprocessed emotions create physiological tension and keep the system on high alert. As emotions are acknowledged and allowed, your nervous system load lightens, and symptoms often ease.

 

5. Weave in Ease, Joy & Play – Positive Neuroplasticity
 Joy, creativity, rest, and play are not “extras” for after you heal—they are part of the medicine. We intentionally build them into your life.

➤ Why it heals: Experiences of ease and pleasure are strong signals of safety. They change your brain chemistry and lay down new neural pathways grounded in trust, connection, and possibility.

Each coaching program is highly individualized—no two pain stories are alike, and your plan won’t be either.

 

WHAT IT'S LIKE TO WORK WITH ME

Clients often describe me as uplifting, supportive, hopeful, a bit nerdy, and endlessly curious.

That curiosity is at the heart of my work: I approach you and your symptoms like a compassionate detective, looking for the unique levers that will help your nervous system shift from threat to safety.

Not everything works for everyone—but together, we will keep exploring until we find what works for you.

  • Sessions: Typically 60 minutes

  • Frequency: Often weekly to start, spread apart once concepts are established

  • Format: Zoom or in-person (Seattle area)

  • Languages: English & German

 

NEXT STEP

I offer a free 30-minute Zoom consultation to explore whether this approach feels like a good fit for you. 

Whether we work together or not, I wish you clarity, hope, and genuine support on your healing journey.

 

OFFERING A ROADMAP BACK TO MORE JOY  - OTHER WAYS TO CONNECT WITH ME

When you live with chronic symptoms, life can feel heavy, and healing can feel lonely. Pain, fatigue, or stress may dim your spark, and joy can seem like a distant memory. Even when you crave more lightness and ease, it’s hard to know where to start.

This is why I created mindbodyJOY - a first-of-its-kind chronic joy community for people living with chronic symptoms. 

At mindbodyJOY, we offer a gentle, science-backed space where you can take small, playful steps to reconnect with joy, creativity, and ease — all within a vibrant, supportive community that makes healing feel lighter and more possible. 

Inside the community, you’ll:

  • Find your Play Personality – take a quiz to learn how you naturally like to play
  • Explore One weekly Joy Quest – a tiny joy experiment to explore for the week
  • Have access to weekly Live Zoom Playdates (creative expression, laughter yoga, and authentic connection)
  • Feel supported by a vibrant community of people who get it
  • Realize that joy is medicine!

 

Through the mindbodyJOY method, our members begin to reframe their relationship with joy. They come to realize that joy isn’t frivolous, optional, or something to be earned after healing — it’s a powerful catalyst of the healing process itself. 

If you are the tiniest bit curious, I invite you to start your FREE 2-Week Trial and begin your journey back to more joy today (link below).

column22:
I’m a licensed psychologist based in Boulder, CO, and I provide in-person and telehealth services to adults across the U.S. I specialize in supporting people living with chronic pain and other neuroplastic symptoms. My approach is relational and psychodynamic, meaning I focus on building a safe, collaborative relationship while exploring the deeper emotional patterns that shape your experience. In our work, I draw from Intensive Short-term Dynamic Psychotherapy
(ISTDP), Relational Psychoanalysis, and Attachment Theory, and I integrate evidence-based approaches such as Emotional Awareness and Expression Therapy (EAET) and Pain Reprocessing Therapy (PRT). My goal is to help you better understand your mind–body connection, reduce suffering, and foster lasting change.

Telehealth in Specific Locations, including California, Colorado, and PsyPact states (as of 9/30/25, all US states except Massachusetts, New York, Montana, New Mexico, Alaska, Hawaii, Iowa, Louisiana, Oregon, Puerto Rico, US Virgin Islands and Guam)
